Alabama.custodyStandard: Alabama courts apply the 'best interests of the child' standard when determining custody. Factors include the child's age, the parents' ability to meet the child's needs, any history of domestic violence or abuse, the child's relationship with each parent, and the child's preference if the child is of sufficient maturity.
Alabama.custodyTypes: Alabama recognizes both legal custody (decision-making authority over education, healthcare, religious upbringing) and physical custody (where the child lives). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is common; joint physical custody requires both parents to be able to cooperate. Sole custody may be granted when joint custody is not in the child's best interests.
Alabama.modificationRules: A custody order can be modified if there has been a material change in circumstances since the last order. The change must be substantial enough to justify a custody modification and the proposed change must serve the child's best interests. Courts examine whether circumstances have materially changed and whether modification benefits the child.
Alabama.relocationRules: A parent seeking to relocate must provide the other parent with at least 45 days advance written notice. The non-relocating parent may file an objection. The court will consider whether the move is in good faith, the impact on the child's relationship with the other parent, and how visitation can be maintained. The burden is on the relocating parent to show the move is in the child's best interests.
Alabama.enforcementOptions: Alabama courts can enforce custody orders through contempt proceedings, which may result in fines or jail time. Courts may also award makeup visitation, attorney fees, and modify custody as a result of repeated violations. Law enforcement can assist in returning a child to the custodial parent. The Uniform Child Custody Jurisdiction and Enforcement Act (UCCJEA) allows for interstate enforcement.

Alaska.custodyStandard: Alaska courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child, considering factors such as the physical, emotional, mental, religious, and social needs of the child; the capability and desire of each parent to meet those needs; the child's preference if of sufficient age; any special needs of the child; the stability of the home environment; and any domestic violence, child abuse, or neglect.
Alaska.custodyTypes: Alaska provides for legal custody (decision-making) and physical custody (residence). Both can be sole or shared. Shared custody can be either shared legal, shared physical, or both. Courts encourage parents to work out parenting plans. If parents cannot agree, the court will determine the custody arrangement based on the child's best interests.
Alaska.modificationRules: To modify custody, a parent must show a substantial change in circumstances that warrants revisiting the arrangement. The court then considers whether modification is in the child's best interests. Alaska has a two-year waiting period before custody modifications can be requested, with exceptions for domestic violence, substance abuse issues, or the child's preference.
Alaska.relocationRules: A parent wishing to relocate must provide 30 days written notice if the move would significantly change the child's contact with the other parent. The non-relocating parent can object. The court will weigh the reasons for the move, the impact on the child's relationship with the non-moving parent, and proposed accommodations for maintaining that relationship.
Alaska.enforcementOptions: Custody orders may be enforced through contempt of court proceedings, resulting in fines or jail time. Courts may also award attorney fees and modify the custody arrangement. Emergency orders are available if the child is in danger. The UCCJEA governs interstate enforcement.

Arizona.custodyStandard: Arizona uses the term 'legal decision-making' for what other states call legal custody, and 'parenting time' for visitation/physical custody. Courts decide based on the best interests of the child, evaluating parental relationships, each parent's past, present, and potential future relationships with the child, the child's adjustment to home, school, and community, mental and physical health of all parties, and any coercion or duress used to obtain an agreement.
Arizona.custodyTypes: Arizona recognizes sole and joint legal decision-making. For parenting time, courts aim to maximize each parent's time with the child unless contrary to the child's best interests. Arizona law presumes joint legal decision-making unless there is evidence of domestic violence. The court can award one parent final decision-making authority over specific matters like education or healthcare.
Arizona.modificationRules: A parent must wait one year after entry of a custody order before seeking modification, unless there is serious endangerment to the child. After one year, modification requires showing a substantial and continuing change in circumstances. The court will again apply the best interests standard.
Arizona.relocationRules: A parent must provide 45 days written notice before relocating more than 100 miles or out of state. The other parent may object, and the court will hold a hearing. Arizona courts consider the relocation's impact on the parenting plan and the child's relationship with the non-relocating parent. The relocating parent bears the burden of proving the move is in the child's best interests.
Arizona.enforcementOptions: Arizona courts can hold a parent in contempt for violating parenting time orders. Remedies include make-up parenting time, civil penalties, attorney fees, community service, or in serious cases, jail time. Courts may also modify the parenting plan if violations are persistent. The UCCJEA governs interstate matters.

California.custodyStandard: California courts determine child custody based on the health, safety, and welfare of the child as the primary standard. Courts strongly favor arrangements that assure the child frequent and continuing contact with both parents. Domestic violence is a significant factor, and courts presume that joint custody is detrimental if a parent has been found to have committed domestic violence.
California.custodyTypes: California recognizes legal custody (decision-making over education, healthcare, religious upbringing) and physical custody (residence). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is common and encourages both parents to share major decisions. Joint physical custody means the child spends substantial time with both parents. Courts may award primary physical custody to one parent with visitation rights to the other.
California.modificationRules: To modify a final custody order, a parent must show a significant change of circumstances. However, for temporary orders or those recently issued, courts apply a 'best interests' test without the change of circumstances requirement. The move-away context has specific rules, and the parent with primary custody bears the burden of showing the move is appropriate.
California.relocationRules: A parent with joint physical custody who wants to move must give 45 days advance written notice and may need to seek court approval if the move would change the current custody arrangement. California courts will consider the extent to which the move affects the existing custodial arrangement and the child's relationship with the non-moving parent. The court applies the 'best interests' standard.
California.enforcementOptions: California courts can enforce custody orders through contempt proceedings, resulting in fines or imprisonment. Make-up visitation may be ordered. The court may modify custody arrangements as a result of willful violations. California has a Child Abduction Unit and law enforcement assistance for parental abduction cases. The UCCJEA applies for interstate enforcement.

Colorado.custodyStandard: Colorado uses the term 'parental responsibilities' and courts allocate both decision-making and parenting time based on the best interests of the child. Factors include the parents' wishes, the child's preferences, the child's relationship with each parent and siblings, the child's adjustment to home, school, and community, the mental and physical health of all individuals, the ability of each parent to encourage love and contact with the other parent, and any evidence of child abuse or domestic violence.
Colorado.custodyTypes: Colorado distinguishes between 'decision-making responsibility' (similar to legal custody) and 'parenting time' (similar to physical custody). Both can be allocated solely or jointly. Joint decision-making is common unless there is a history of domestic violence or abuse. Parenting time schedules are detailed in a parenting plan that outlines each parent's time with the child.
Colorado.modificationRules: Colorado requires a two-year waiting period before modifying parenting time unless circumstances endanger the child. After two years, substantial change in circumstances is required. Modification of decision-making also requires a change in circumstances and that the modification serves the child's best interests.
Colorado.relocationRules: A parent with primary parenting time must provide 91 days written notice before relocating with the child. The non-relocating parent may object. The court will consider the reasons for the relocation, the quality of the relationships involved, the impact on the child, and whether a modified schedule can preserve the non-relocating parent's relationship with the child.
Colorado.enforcementOptions: Colorado courts enforce parenting time and decision-making orders through contempt proceedings. Remedies include makeup parenting time, civil fines, attorney fees, or modification of the existing order. Courts may also issue bench warrants for parents who repeatedly violate orders. Interstate enforcement is governed by the UCCJEA.

Florida.custodyStandard: Florida uses the term 'parental responsibility' and 'time-sharing' instead of custody. Courts determine arrangements based on the best interests of the child. Florida has 20 statutory factors including the demonstrated capacity of each parent to facilitate a close parent-child relationship, the division of parental responsibilities, any history of domestic violence, the geographical viability of the plan, and the moral fitness of the parents.
Florida.custodyTypes: Florida provides for 'shared parental responsibility' (similar to joint legal custody) which is the default, and 'sole parental responsibility' in exceptional cases. Time-sharing schedules determine where and when the child spends time with each parent. Florida recognizes equal time-sharing as a starting point, but this can be adjusted based on best interests factors and the statutory 20-factor test.
Florida.modificationRules: Florida requires a substantial, material, and unanticipated change in circumstances to modify a time-sharing schedule or parental responsibility. Additionally, the modification must be in the child's best interests. This high standard protects stability for children.
Florida.relocationRules: Florida requires parents with time-sharing to provide notice and obtain consent or court approval for relocations more than 50 miles from the primary residence. Parents must file a petition with the court, and both parties will present evidence regarding the potential impact on the child. The relocating parent bears the burden of demonstrating the move is in the child's best interests.
Florida.enforcementOptions: Florida courts enforce time-sharing orders through contempt, which can result in fines, community service, or jail time. Courts may order makeup time-sharing, require the violating parent to pay attorney fees, or modify the time-sharing plan. Law enforcement can assist in returning a child to the appropriate parent. The UCCJEA applies for interstate situations.

Georgia.custodyStandard: Georgia courts award custody based on the best interests of the child. Key factors include each parent's love, affection, and emotional ties with the child; the willingness of each parent to support the other's relationship with the child; each parent's ability to provide for the child's basic needs; the home environment of each parent; the stability of family life for the child; each parent's knowledge and familiarity with the child; and any evidence of domestic violence or substance abuse.
Georgia.custodyTypes: Georgia recognizes legal custody (decision-making over education, healthcare, religious upbringing) and physical custody (where the child lives). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody allows both parents to share major decisions. Physical custody can be primary (child primarily lives with one parent) or joint (child splits time between both parents). Georgia courts may also consider the child's preference if the child is 14 or older.
Georgia.modificationRules: To modify a Georgia custody order, a parent must show a material change in circumstances affecting the welfare of the child since the last order. If the child is 14 or older, the child has the right to select the parent with whom they prefer to live, subject to the court's approval. For children between 11-13, the court considers their preference but it is not binding.
Georgia.relocationRules: A parent with primary physical custody must provide 30 days advance written notice to the other parent and the court before relocating to another state or more than 50 miles from the other parent within Georgia. The non-relocating parent can petition the court to prevent the move or modify custody. The court will evaluate whether the move is in the child's best interests.
Georgia.enforcementOptions: Custody orders in Georgia can be enforced through contempt of court proceedings. A parent who willfully violates a custody order may face fines, attorney fees, and in serious cases, jail time. Courts may also modify custody as a consequence of repeated violations. Georgia law allows for the filing of a 'writ of habeas corpus' to enforce custody rights.

Illinois.custodyStandard: Illinois uses the term 'allocation of parental responsibilities' which replaced the traditional custody terminology. Courts allocate responsibilities based on the best interests of the child. Factors include the child's wishes, the parents' wishes, the child's adjustment to home, school, and community, the mental and physical health of all parties, the ability of parents to cooperate, and any history of domestic violence or abuse.
Illinois.custodyTypes: Illinois allocates 'significant decision-making responsibilities' (similar to legal custody) and 'parenting time' (similar to physical custody). Significant decisions include education, healthcare, extracurricular activities, and religious upbringing. Both parenting time and decision-making can be shared or allocated primarily to one parent.
Illinois.modificationRules: Illinois has a two-year waiting period before modifications can be requested unless the child's present environment seriously endangers the child. After two years, modification requires showing a substantial change of circumstances and that modification serves the child's best interests. For parenting time modifications (not decision-making), courts apply a lower threshold.
Illinois.relocationRules: A parent with majority parenting time who wants to relocate with the child must provide 60 days written notice. If the other parent objects, the relocating parent must file a petition with the court. Relocations within Illinois of 25 miles or more (for the Chicago area) or 50 miles or more (other areas), or any relocation to another state, require notice or court approval.
Illinois.enforcementOptions: Illinois courts enforce parenting time orders through contempt proceedings. Remedies include makeup parenting time (up to twice the denied time), attorney fees, and modification of the parenting plan. Courts can issue civil warrants for parents who repeatedly deny parenting time. The UCCJEA governs interstate enforcement.

New York.custodyStandard: New York courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. There are no specific statutory factors, but courts consider parental fitness, the home environment, the child's relationship with each parent, the quality of each parent's parenting, the parent's ability to provide for the child's emotional, intellectual, and physical needs, and any history of domestic violence or substance abuse. New York courts also consider the preference of older children.
New York.custodyTypes: New York recognizes legal custody (decision-making authority) and physical custody (where the child lives). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is common but requires the parents to be able to communicate and cooperate. Joint physical custody means substantial time with both parents. Courts can craft highly individualized arrangements based on the specific family's circumstances.
New York.modificationRules: To modify a New York custody order, a parent must show a substantial change in circumstances since the last order and that modification would serve the child's best interests. Courts also consider stability and continuity for the child. Changes such as a parent's relocation, a significant change in a parent's lifestyle, or the child's own changing needs may qualify as substantial changes.
New York.relocationRules: New York has no specific statute requiring advance notice of relocation. However, courts balance the custodial parent's interest in moving with the non-custodial parent's interest in maintaining a relationship with the child. The relocating parent must demonstrate that the move is in the child's best interests based on factors like the reason for the move, the impact on the child's relationship with the other parent, and quality of life improvements.
New York.enforcementOptions: New York courts enforce custody orders through family offense proceedings and contempt citations. Violations can result in modification of custody, fines, jail time, and attorney fee awards. Law enforcement can assist in enforcing custody orders. Courts may issue pickup orders if a child is improperly retained. Interstate enforcement is governed by the UCCJEA.

Texas.custodyStandard: Texas uses the terms 'conservatorship' (custody) and 'possession and access' (visitation). Courts determine arrangements based on the best interests of the child. Factors include the child's physical and emotional needs, each parent's abilities, stability of home environment, any domestic violence or abuse history, the child's relationship with each parent, and the parents' ability to place the child's needs above their own.
Texas.custodyTypes: Texas provides for 'joint managing conservatorship' (similar to joint legal custody) and 'sole managing conservatorship.' Joint managing conservatorship is the legal presumption and means both parents share rights and duties, though not necessarily equal possession time. Sole managing conservatorship is awarded when joint is contrary to the best interests of the child, typically when there is domestic violence or abuse.
Texas.modificationRules: To modify a Texas custody order, you must show a material and substantial change in circumstances affecting the child or a conservator since the last order, or that the child is at least 12 years old and has expressed a preference to the court. The modification must also be in the child's best interests. Texas has a one-year waiting period for most modifications.
Texas.relocationRules: Texas custody orders often include a geographic restriction that limits where the child can live (often the county where the suit was filed plus contiguous counties). A parent wanting to move outside this area must petition the court or get consent from the other parent. The court will evaluate whether the move is in the child's best interests and how visitation can be maintained.
Texas.enforcementOptions: Texas courts enforce custody orders through motions for enforcement and contempt, which can result in fines, community service, jail time, and makeup possession time. Courts can also modify custody arrangements as a result of violations. Texas has specific enforcement statutes that allow for attorney fee awards. The UCCJEA governs interstate enforcement.

Washington.custodyStandard: Washington uses the term 'parenting plan' to govern custody arrangements. Courts determine plans based on the best interests of the child. Factors include the strength of the child's relationships with each parent, each parent's past and potential future performance of parenting functions, emotional climate in each home, the child's cultural background and community ties, and any history of domestic violence or abuse.
Washington.custodyTypes: Washington establishes a residential schedule detailing when the child is with each parent, and allocates decision-making authority for major decisions. Decision-making can be mutual (joint) or reserved for one parent for specified areas. Residential arrangements range from primary residence with one parent to equal time-sharing, based on what serves the child's best interests.
Washington.modificationRules: Washington allows modification of parenting plans when there is a substantial change in circumstances of the child or a parent. Courts will not consider modifications unless the child has been integrated into the family of the petitioner with consent, or the child's present environment is detrimental. The court applies the best interests standard after finding the threshold is met.
Washington.relocationRules: Washington requires 60 days written notice before a parent with primary residential time relocates with a child. The other parent can object. If there is an objection, the relocating parent must file a petition, and the court will evaluate the move. Washington courts consider the relative impact of relocation versus non-relocation on the child's development.
Washington.enforcementOptions: Washington courts enforce parenting plans through contempt of court. Violations can result in fines, attorney fees, makeup residential time, and modification of the parenting plan. Courts can also require violating parents to attend parenting classes. Law enforcement can assist in enforcing residential schedules. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.

Ohio.custodyStandard: Ohio courts award custody based on the best interests of the child. Relevant factors include the wishes of the child's parents, the child's wishes if they are of an appropriate age, the child's interaction and interrelationship with parents and siblings, the child's adjustment to home, school, and community, the mental and physical health of all parties, the parent more likely to honor and facilitate visitation rights, whether child support is being paid, and any history of domestic violence or drug abuse.
Ohio.custodyTypes: Ohio recognizes 'legal custody' (decision-making authority) and 'physical custody' (residential placement). Both can be sole or joint (called 'shared parenting' in Ohio). Shared parenting requires both parents to file a shared parenting plan; if only one parent requests it, the court may still order it if it is in the child's best interests. Courts can also designate a 'residential parent' for school purposes.
Ohio.modificationRules: Ohio requires a change in circumstances to modify a custody order; however, if the parents have shared parenting, modifications are slightly more flexible. The change must affect the child, and modification must be in the child's best interests. Ohio courts apply different standards depending on whether there is shared parenting or primary custody.
Ohio.relocationRules: Ohio requires parents with residential custody to notify the court and the other parent when planning to relocate. The non-residential parent may file a motion for reallocation of parental rights and responsibilities. Courts consider the reason for the move, the impact on the child's relationship with the other parent, and proposed arrangements for maintaining contact.
Ohio.enforcementOptions: Ohio courts enforce custody orders through contempt proceedings, which can result in fines or jail time. Courts may order makeup parenting time and attorney fee awards. Courts can also modify custody arrangements if violations are persistent. The Ohio UCCJEA governs interstate enforcement.

Pennsylvania.custodyStandard: Pennsylvania courts award custody based on the best interests of the child, applying 16 statutory factors. These include the parties' potential to encourage close and continuing contact between the child and the other party; the present and past abuse; the parental duties performed by each party; the need for stability and continuity in the child's education, family life, and community life; the availability of extended family; the child's sibling relationships; well-reasoned preference of the child based on maturity and judgment; and any evidence of domestic violence.
Pennsylvania.custodyTypes: Pennsylvania provides for 'legal custody' (decision-making about important decisions) and 'physical custody' (where the child lives). Each can be sole or shared. Shared legal custody is common when parents can cooperate. Physical custody arrangements include primary physical custody (child primarily with one parent), shared physical custody (child spends substantial time with both), and partial physical custody or supervised physical custody.
Pennsylvania.modificationRules: Pennsylvania courts can modify custody orders when there has been a substantial change in circumstances. Courts will then apply the best interests standard. Unlike some states, Pennsylvania does not have a waiting period for modifications. However, courts do consider the child's stability and continuity when evaluating changes.
Pennsylvania.relocationRules: Pennsylvania requires a parent seeking to relocate to provide notice to the other parent and all parties with custody rights. The notice must be served 60 days in advance and include specific information about the proposed move. The other parent can object by filing a counter-affidavit. If there is an objection, the court holds a hearing and determines whether relocation is in the child's best interests.
Pennsylvania.enforcementOptions: Pennsylvania courts enforce custody orders through contempt proceedings. A parent who violates a custody order may be held in contempt, resulting in fines, imprisonment, makeup custody time, and attorney fees. Courts can also modify custody arrangements as a consequence. Interstate enforcement is governed by the UCCJEA.

Michigan.custodyStandard: Michigan courts award custody based on the best interests of the child using 12 statutory factors. These include emotional ties between the child and each parent, the capacity of each parent to give the child love and guidance, the capacity of each parent to provide for material needs, the length of time the child has lived in a stable environment, the permanence of the family unit, the moral fitness of the parents, the mental and physical health of the parties, the child's school record, the child's preference if of an appropriate age, the willingness of each parent to facilitate visitation, and any evidence of domestic violence.
Michigan.custodyTypes: Michigan recognizes legal custody (decision-making authority) and physical custody (residential placement). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is presumed to be in the child's best interests unless one parent is deemed unfit. Joint physical custody means the child spends substantial time with both parents. Michigan courts may award primary physical custody to one parent with parenting time (visitation) to the other.
Michigan.modificationRules: To modify a Michigan custody order, a parent must first show proper cause or a change of circumstances. If shown, the court then considers whether the change warrants revisiting the best interests factors. Michigan also recognizes a 'established custodial environment,' which, if changed, requires a higher burden of proof (clear and convincing evidence).
Michigan.relocationRules: Michigan requires a parent with custody of a minor child to obtain court approval or the other parent's consent before moving the child's domicile more than 100 miles from the original location. Courts will apply factors specific to relocation, including the reasons for the proposed move, the other parent's reasons for opposing, and the child's relationships with each parent.
Michigan.enforcementOptions: Michigan courts enforce custody orders through contempt proceedings, which can result in fines or incarceration. Courts may order makeup parenting time and attorney fees. If a parent repeatedly violates orders, the court may modify custody arrangements. Law enforcement can assist with enforcement. The UCCJEA governs interstate cases.

North Carolina.custodyStandard: North Carolina courts determine custody based on the best interests and welfare of the child. While there are no specific statutory factors (unlike some states), courts consider the fitness of each parent, the quality of each parent's relationship with the child, each parent's ability to provide for the child, stability and continuity in the child's life, the child's relationship with siblings, any history of domestic violence or abuse, and the preference of older children.
North Carolina.custodyTypes: North Carolina provides for legal custody (decision-making authority) and physical custody (where the child lives). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody allows both parents to share major decisions regarding the child. Joint physical custody means the child spends substantial time with both parents. Courts craft custody orders tailored to each family's unique circumstances.
North Carolina.modificationRules: North Carolina allows modification of custody orders when there has been a substantial change in circumstances affecting the welfare of the child. The court then applies the best interests standard. Examples of substantial changes include a significant change in a parent's lifestyle, a new relationship that affects the child, relocation, or the child's changing needs.
North Carolina.relocationRules: North Carolina does not have a specific relocation statute, but custody orders typically include provisions regarding relocation. If a parent wants to relocate and it would affect the custody arrangement, the other parent may petition the court for modification. Courts evaluate the best interests of the child considering the proposed move.
North Carolina.enforcementOptions: North Carolina courts enforce custody orders through contempt of court. Violations can result in fines, attorney fees, makeup visitation, and modification of the custody arrangement. Courts can also issue bench warrants in serious cases. Interstate enforcement is governed by the UCCJEA.

Virginia.custodyStandard: Virginia courts determine child custody and visitation based on the best interests of the child. Statutory factors include the age and physical and mental condition of the child and parents; the relationship between each parent and child; the needs of the child; the role played by each parent in the upbringing of the child; the propensity of each parent to actively support the child's contact with the other parent; the willingness to resolve disputes by mutual agreement; the reasonable preference of the child if the court deems the child to be of reasonable intelligence and maturity; and any history of family abuse.
Virginia.custodyTypes: Virginia provides for legal custody (decision-making authority) and physical custody (residential arrangement). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is favored when parents can cooperate. Joint physical custody means the child spends substantial time with both parents. Courts may award primary physical custody to one parent with visitation to the other. Courts can also order supervised visitation if necessary for the child's safety.
Virginia.modificationRules: Virginia allows modification of custody orders upon a showing of a material change in circumstances and that modification would serve the child's best interests. Material changes include significant changes in a parent's life circumstances, relocation, a parent's remarriage, or the child's changing needs.
Virginia.relocationRules: Virginia does not have a specific relocation statute, but courts impose relocation restrictions in custody orders. A parent wishing to relocate must either obtain the other parent's consent or court approval. Courts consider the impact of the proposed move on the child's relationship with both parents and whether the relocation is in the child's best interests.
Virginia.enforcementOptions: Virginia courts enforce custody orders through civil contempt proceedings. Violations can result in fines, attorney fees, makeup visitation, and modification of custody. Courts can also hold parents criminally liable for custodial interference. Law enforcement can assist with enforcement. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
